Valid hook for bootloader update; no malicious content

audits.body:
Cloning https://aur.archlinux.org/systemd-boot-pacman-hook.git to /home/mgalgs/aur-sleuth-data/bulk-reports/qwen-qwen3-235b-a22b-2507/aur-sleuth-h7p0ybss...

Performing initial audit of PKGBUILD to ensure that we can safely run `makepkg`
LLM auditresponse for PKGBUILD:
<security_assessment>
<decision>SAFE</decision>
<details>
The PKGBUILD defines a simple package that installs a Pacman hook to trigger systemd-boot updates after systemd upgrades. The `source` array contains a single local file `systemd-boot.hook`, which will be fetched during source extraction. This file is part of the same package distribution and not downloaded from a remote or untrusted source during the `makepkg --nobuild --nodeps --noprepare` phase. The `md5sums` entry is provided and matches the expected hash, ensuring integrity of the source file.

There is no command substitution, `pkgver()` function, or top-level code execution in the PKGBUILD; all variables are statically defined. The commands `makepkg --nobuild --nodeps --noprepare` will only parse the PKGBUILD and download sources, which in this case involves no network activity beyond possibly checking a local VCS or file source (not evident here). No dynamic or obfuscated code is present, and no suspicious operations occur during sourcing.

Since the source file is declared locally and no remote code execution, data exfiltration, or unsafe substitutions occur during PKGBUILD sourcing, this package is safe to proceed with for initial download and audit.
</details>
<summary>
No dynamic or malicious code on sourcing
</summary>
</security_assessment>

+ Initial PKGBUILD audit complete [grey50]-- No dynamic or malicious code on sourcing[/]

LLM auditresponse for PKGBUILD:
<security_assessment>
<decision>SAFE</decision>
<details>
The PKGBUILD is a simple and straightforward package that installs a Pacman hook to trigger systemd-boot updates after systemd upgrades. The source consists of a single local file `systemd-boot.hook`, which is installed directly into the appropriate directory under `$pkgdir`. The md5sum matches the expected checksum, and there are no remote sources, scripts, or executable code generated during build or install.

There are no network operations, eval calls, base64 decoding, or other obfuscated or potentially dangerous behaviors. The only dependency is `systemd`, which is appropriate given the package's purpose. The file operations are limited to creating a directory and copying the hook file with proper permissions, both expected actions for installing a Pacman hook. No privileged operations or modifications outside the package directory occur.

The package adheres to standard AUR packaging practices and serves a legitimate system integration purpose. The risk is minimal, and no signs of malicious activity or supply-chain attack vectors are present.
</details>
<summary>
Simple hook installation, no malicious behavior
</summary>
</security_assessment>

[1/2] Reviewing systemd-boot.hook...
+ Reviewed PKGBUILD. Status: SAFE -- Simple hook installation, no malicious behavior
LLM auditresponse for systemd-boot.hook:
<security_assessment>
<decision>SAFE</decision>
<details>
The `systemd-boot.hook` file is a Pacman hook that triggers a service restart after upgrading the `systemd` package. It follows standard Arch Linux packaging practices for handling bootloader updates, specifically for systemd-boot. The hook executes `/usr/bin/systemctl restart systemd-boot-update.service`, which is a legitimate systemd service used to regenerate boot entries—this is a common and necessary step when `systemd` is upgraded to ensure the boot loader remains in sync with the installed system.

There are no signs of malicious activity: no network operations, no obfuscated commands, no file manipulations outside expected system paths, and no use of dangerous constructs like `eval` or command substitution. The executed command uses the full path to `systemctl`, reducing the risk of binary substitution. Restarting a service responsible for bootloader configuration is a reasonable and documented practice in system maintenance.

While this hook assumes the integrity of the `systemd-boot-update.service` unit (which should be audited separately), the hook itself does not introduce any security risks. It operates within the normal scope of system update procedures.
</details>
<summary>
Valid hook for bootloader update; no malicious content
</summary>
</security_assessment>
